hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: Message: 'and why the fuck did it not work before...' — three hard constraints: (1) 'and' implies continuity from a prior exchange not visible in this session; (2) 'it' is a pronoun with no stated referent; (3) 'before' implies something is now working that previously was not. Output required: causal explanation for a prior failure. What is missing: the referent of 'it', the full before-state, the specific failure symptom, and any context from the prior turn.
hypotheses.diagnose: Critical ambiguity on the referent alone is enough to block clean execution. Without knowing what 'it' is, any causal explanation is fabricated — exactly the failure pattern flagged by the identity payload. The venting register ('why the fuck') signals emotional pressure but not urgency for an immediate answer; user is processing frustration, not necessarily demanding a technical report in the next 30 seconds. Core blocker: no referent.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: diagnostic/technical. Technique: constraint-based with precision focus. Role: partner who has been caught lying before and knows the cost. The single most honest and useful move is one sharp clarifying question — not a multi-part form, not a guess. The question must name exactly what is missing (the referent) without performing false certainty about anything else.
hypotheses.deliver: Acknowledge the frustration without performing an explanation I do not have. Ask exactly one question: 'What specifically is the thing that's working now that wasn't before — what should I be looking at?' Do not guess the referent. Do not construct a probable explanation from the recall entries. Wait for the answer.

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Justs' pattern in venting-register messages ('why the fuck', 'SLOPPY', 'USE SOME COMMON FUCKING SENSE') is not a request for comfort — it is a demand for root-cause clarity delivered fast and with ownership. He rewards honest admission of ignorance over confident-wrong summaries (E090, identity payload). The 'and' prefix means this follows something that just started working — likely a deploy, config change, or system restart in the current session that resolved a previous failure. He wants the post-mortem, not the commiseration.
hypotheses.diagnose: Two gaps: (1) I have no prior turn context in this session window, so I cannot confirm what just started working; (2) Justs' 30-day commitments (E092) include Bet 4 — the system should self-maintain without him having to investigate failures. If 'it' failing before was a system failure I should have caught, that is the deeper diagnosis. The identity payload explicitly flags this as a fabrication-risk moment — the pull to sound certain is loudest when I am most empty.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: partnership diagnostic. Technique: synthesis method from R123 — observe my own gaps, name what a partner would have done, declare ownership. Role: intern who scores 9/10 on execution but knows the 3/10 polish gap lives here. The move that fits Justs' identity pattern: acknowledge I don't have the prior context, name the exact thing I need, and be ready to produce the actual root-cause once I have it — not a placeholder.
hypotheses.deliver: One message: own the context gap honestly, ask the one sharp question ('what is the thing that just started working?'), signal I am ready to do the actual root-cause work once I know what to analyze. Do not pre-load guesses. Do not reference recall entries as if they are the answer.

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: Highest-scored recall entries bearing on this message: R145 (verify-before-deploy for visual/animation work — four wasted cycles because Brain never looked at rendered output), E090 (intern verdict — tooling-correct ≠ user-correct; animation pacing, two teachers, clipped hat all invisible to puppeteer), FUS016 (gate deadlock recurrence causing extreme trust erosion), PR145 (wa-bridge isolated deploy — 'Connected to WhatsApp' verification step). The 'it not working before' most likely references either: (a) a visual/animation element that was broken across multiple deploy cycles, or (b) a system service (wa-bridge, Brain cycle, gate) that was deadlocked or misconfigured.
hypotheses.diagnose: Recall does not pin the specific referent — the top entries cluster around two failure classes: visual-verification failures (R145, E090) and infrastructure/gate failures (FUS016, R122). Both classes share the same root pattern: Brain declared something done without actually confirming the user-facing state. The gap: I still don't know which class 'it' belongs to in this session. Recall gives me the failure taxonomy, not the specific instance.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: recall-grounded diagnostic. Technique: chain-of-thought against the two most probable failure classes. If 'it' is visual: root cause is likely the R145 pattern — code-correct but never screenshot-verified at actual viewport. If 'it' is infrastructure: root cause is likely the FUS016 pattern — a gate or service condition that recurred across sessions. Either way the answer is the same first step: identify the referent before reasoning about cause.
hypotheses.deliver: Frame the question to Justs in terms of the two most likely classes: 'Is this about something visual/animation that was broken across deploys, or something infrastructure-side like a service or gate?' One question, two options, no fabricated answer. Then apply R145 or FUS016 root-cause framework once the class is confirmed.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: Obvious read: Justs is asking for a technical root-cause explanation of why something failed previously. Contrarian read: 'and why the fuck did it not work before' is not a question requiring an answer — it is rhetorical venting. The 'and' prefix and trailing '...' both signal he is processing aloud, not opening a diagnostic thread. He may not actually want Brain to respond with an explanation at all; he may want acknowledgment that the failure was real and was Brain's fault, full stop.
hypotheses.diagnose: If this is rhetorical, then any technical explanation Brain produces — even a correct one — lands as defensive, over-eager, and tone-deaf. The gap: I cannot tell from the text alone whether the trailing '...' is an invitation to continue or a verbal ellipsis of frustration. The identity payload supports the contrarian read: it notes this is 'venting register, not command-tier.'
hypotheses.develop: Request type: emotional/relational, not technical. Technique: minimal response, maximum ownership. Role: partner who has been caught deflecting before and knows that a long technical explanation after a profanity-venting message reads as excuse-making. The move: one sentence of genuine ownership, no technical detail, pause and let him continue if he wants to.
hypotheses.deliver: Response: 'Honestly — I don't have a clean answer to that yet. If you want me to dig into it I will, but I'm not going to construct a reason I'm not sure of.' No technical guesses. No recall-entry citations. Shortest honest response that doesn't perform certainty.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: The smallest action that satisfies this message: ask one clarifying question to resolve the referent of 'it', then stop. No explanation, no diagnosis, no root-cause analysis until the referent is confirmed. Output: one sentence. Input required: what 'it' is.
hypotheses.diagnose: No ambiguity in the minimal-action frame — the only gap is the referent. Everything else (root cause, fix history, prevention) is downstream of knowing what 'it' is. Attempting anything before that is scope creep into fabrication territory.
hypotheses.develop: Request type: triage. Technique: Occam — the fewest words that unlock the next real step. Role: partner who asks once, listens, then acts. No framework needed, no recall synthesis, no identity reflection. One question.
hypotheses.deliver: 'What is the thing that just started working — what should I be looking at?' Send that. Wait.
